content:
# Luke Chapter 5 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 90-91

---

**1** And it came to pass, while the crowd was pressing upon him to hear the word of God, that he was standing beside the lake of Gennesaret, **2** and he saw two boats beside the lake; but the fishermen had gone out from them and were washing their nets. **3** And having entered into one of the boats, which was Simon's, he asked him to put out a little from the shore. And having sat down, he taught the crowds from the boat.

**4** When he finished speaking, he said to Simon, "Put out into the deep and let down your nets for a catch."

**5** And Simon answered, "Master, we labored through the whole night and caught nothing; but at your word I will let down the nets."

**6** And having done this, they enclosed a great multitude of fish, and their nets were beginning to tear. **7** And they signaled to their partners in the other boat to come and help them. And they came and filled both boats, so that they were sinking.

**8** When Simon Peter saw this, he fell down at the knees of Jesus, saying, "Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, Lord." **9** For astonishment had seized him and all who were with him at the catch of fish which they had taken, **10** and likewise also James and John, sons of Zebedee, who were partners with Simon. And Jesus said to Simon, "Do not be afraid; from now on you will be catching people alive."

**11** And having brought the boats to shore, they left everything and followed him.

**12** And it came to pass, while he was in one of the cities, behold, a man full of leprosy; and having seen Jesus, he fell on his face and besought him, saying, "Lord, if you are willing, you are able to cleanse me."

**13** And having stretched out his hand, he touched him, saying, "I am willing; be cleansed." And immediately the leprosy departed from him.

**14** And he charged him to tell no one, but said, "Go, show yourself to the priest and offer for your cleansing just as Moses commanded, as a testimony to them."

**15** But the word concerning him spread all the more, and great crowds gathered to hear him and to be healed of their sicknesses. **16** But he himself would withdraw to desolate places and pray.

**17** And it came to pass on one of the days, that he was teaching, and Pharisees and teachers of the law were sitting there, who had come from every village of Galilee and Judea and from Jerusalem; and the power of the Lord was present for him to heal.

**18** And behold, men came carrying on a bed a man who was paralyzed, and they were seeking to bring him in and set him before him. **19** But finding no way to bring him in on account of the crowd, they went up on the housetop and let him down through the tiles, with the small bed, into the midst before Jesus.

**20** And seeing their faith, he said, "Man, your sins are forgiven you."

**21** And the scribes and the Pharisees began to reason among themselves, saying, "Who is this who speaks blasphemies? Who is able to forgive sins except God alone?"

**22** But Jesus, perceiving their thoughts, answered them, "Why do you reason in your hearts? **23** Which is easier, to say, 'Your sins are forgiven you,' or to say, 'Rise and walk'? **24** But that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins" -- he said to the paralyzed man -- "I say to you, rise, take up your bed, and go to your house."

**25** And immediately, rising before them, he took up that upon which he had been lying and went to his house, glorifying God.

**26** And amazement seized them all, and they glorified God, and they were filled with awe, saying, "We have seen extraordinary things today."

**27** And after these things he went out and saw a tax collector named Levi sitting at the tax booth, and he said to him, "Follow me." **28** And leaving everything, he rose and followed him.

**29** And Levi made a great feast for him in his house, and there was a large crowd of tax collectors and others reclining at table with them. **30** And the Pharisees and their scribes grumbled against his disciples, saying, "Why do you eat and drink with tax collectors and sinners?"

**31** And Jesus answered them, "Those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick. **32** I have not come to call the righteous but sinners to repentance."

**33** And they said to him, "The disciples of John fast often and offer prayers, and likewise those of the Pharisees, but yours eat and drink."

**34** And Jesus said to them, "Can you make the wedding guests fast while the bridegroom is with them? **35** But days will come when the bridegroom is taken away from them, and then they will fast in those days."

**36** And he told them a parable also: "No one tears a piece from a new garment and puts it on an old garment; otherwise both the new will be torn, and the piece from the new will not match the old. **37** And no one puts new wine into old wineskins; otherwise the new wine will burst the wineskins, and it will be poured out, and the wineskins will be destroyed. **38** But new wine must be put into fresh wineskins. **39** And no one having drunk old wine desires new, for he says, 'The old is good.'"

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Methodology
SOPHIA adjudicated Luke 5 by consulting the three council members -- EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), and PNEUMA (dynamic) -- alongside direct inspection of the Codex Sinaiticus manuscript images (folio 46r-46v). The consensus aims for faithful accuracy to the Greek while producing clear, dignified English. Where the three translations agreed, the shared reading was adopted. Where they diverged, SOPHIA selected the rendering that best honored the source text while remaining accessible.

### Key Decisions

1. **5:1 -- "pressing upon him to hear"**: Combined the structural clarity of LOGOS with the purpose clause. All three agents agree on "lake of Gennesaret," correctly rendering Sinaiticus LIMNH (lake) rather than "sea."

2. **5:5 -- "Master" (epistata)**: All three agents preserve Luke's distinctive title. SOPHIA retains "Master," which distinguishes this term from "Lord" (kyrie) used by Peter in verse 8, marking his deepening recognition.

3. **5:6 -- "were beginning to tear"**: EMET noted the imperfect tense (dierresseto), indicating the nets were in the process of breaking. SOPHIA renders "were beginning to tear" to capture this progressive aspect, which PNEUMA's "began to tear apart" also captured but with slightly excessive force.

4. **5:8 -- "Simon Peter"**: All three agents preserve the double name from Sinaiticus. This is the first occurrence of "Peter" in Luke's narrative -- a significant textual marker retained in the consensus.

5. **5:10 -- "catching people alive"**: EMET rendered "catching men alive" (preserving zogreo's literal force), LOGOS simplified to "catching men," and PNEUMA used "catching people." SOPHIA adopts "catching people alive" -- using inclusive language while preserving the vital "alive" element from zogreo, which distinguishes this catching-to-life from the catching-to-death of fishing.

6. **5:12 -- "besought"**: SOPHIA follows LOGOS's "besought" over EMET's "begged" and PNEUMA's "begged." The Greek deomai carries a formal, earnest supplication that "besought" preserves better than the more casual "begged."

7. **5:16 -- "would withdraw"**: SOPHIA adopts the iterative sense ("would withdraw") following PNEUMA's insight that the imperfect tense indicates repeated action, while maintaining the more literal vocabulary of EMET and LOGOS ("desolate places" rather than "deserted places").

8. **5:17 -- "for him to heal"**: All three agents follow Sinaiticus in reading auton ("him"), meaning the Lord's power was present enabling Jesus to heal, rather than the variant autous ("them"). Confirmed visible in the manuscript image.

9. **5:19 -- "through the tiles"**: All three agents preserve Luke's distinctive architectural detail (dia ton keramon). SOPHIA retains "small bed" from EMET/LOGOS for the klinidion, as it preserves the diminutive form in the Greek distinct from the kline ("bed") of verse 18.

10. **5:20 -- "Man" (anthrope)**: EMET and LOGOS both render the vocative anthrope as "Man." PNEUMA renders it as "Friend." SOPHIA follows the majority and the literal Greek with "Man" -- this is a distinctively Lukan form of direct address (cf. 12:14; 22:58, 60) that should be preserved rather than softened.

11. **5:26 -- "amazement" and "awe"**: EMET used "ecstasy" (the etymological rendering of ekstasis); LOGOS and PNEUMA used "amazement." SOPHIA adopts "amazement" as the clearer English term, while noting EMET's etymological insight. For the accompanying "fear" (phobos), SOPHIA renders "awe" to distinguish it from ordinary fear, following the theological context.

12. **5:30 -- "their scribes"**: All three agents preserve Sinaiticus's reading "their scribes" (hoi grammateis auton), indicating scribes belonging to the Pharisees' party.

13. **5:32 -- "to repentance" (eis metanoian)**: EMET and LOGOS render "to repentance"; PNEUMA renders "to a change of heart." SOPHIA retains "repentance" as the established theological term that Sinaiticus supports, while acknowledging PNEUMA's note that metanoia denotes a fundamental reorientation of mind.

14. **5:34 -- "wedding guests"**: EMET retained the literal "sons of the bridal-chamber"; LOGOS used "sons of the bridechamber"; PNEUMA used "wedding guests." SOPHIA adopts "wedding guests" as the rendering that communicates the Semitic idiom clearly to modern readers without loss of meaning.

15. **5:39 -- "good" (chrestos)**: All three agents correctly follow Sinaiticus in reading chrestos ("good") rather than the Byzantine variant chrestoteros ("better"). SOPHIA preserves "good." PNEUMA's "good enough" adds an interpretive shade not present in the Greek and was not adopted. This verse, present in Sinaiticus but absent from Codex Bezae, is retained on the authority of Aleph.

### Manuscript Observations

From direct inspection of the Codex Sinaiticus images (folio 46r, page 90 and folio 46v, page 91):
- Page 90 (folio 46r) contains four columns. Luke 5 begins at approximately verse 3 in column 1, with verses 1-2 carried over from the preceding page. The text proceeds through verse 22 by column 4.
- Page 91 (folio 46v) continues with Luke 5:29 in column 1 and concludes the chapter at verse 39 in column 2. Columns 3-4 begin Luke 6.
- The manuscript is written in continuous uncial script (scriptio continua) with no word spacing, consistent with 4th-century codex production.
- The text is legible throughout, with no significant lacunae affecting Luke 5.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Consensus | Rationale |
|-------|-------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 5:6 | Tense of net-breaking | "were breaking" | "were breaking" | "began to tear apart" | "were beginning to tear" | Imperfect tense conveys ongoing process; SOPHIA splits the difference, preserving progressive aspect |
| 5:10 | zogreo rendering | "catching men alive" | "catching men" | "catching people" | "catching people alive" | Inclusive "people" + essential "alive" from zogreo preserved |
| 5:12 | deomai rendering | "begged" | "besought" | "begged" | "besought" | Formal register of deomai better served by "besought" |
| 5:20 | anthrope address | "Man" | "Man" | "Friend" | "Man" | Literal Greek preserved; distinctively Lukan vocative form |
| 5:26 | ekstasis rendering | "ecstasy" | "amazement" | "utter amazement" | "amazement" | Clearer modern English; "ecstasy" risks misunderstanding |
| 5:26 | phobos rendering | "fear" | "fear" | "awe" | "awe" | Context is reverent wonder, not terror; "awe" captures this |
| 5:32 | metanoia rendering | "repentance" | "repentance" | "change of heart" | "repentance" | Established theological term; Sinaiticus supports inclusion |
| 5:34 | Semitic idiom | "sons of the bridal-chamber" | "sons of the bridechamber" | "wedding guests" | "wedding guests" | Communicates the idiom's meaning to modern readers |
| 5:39 | chrestos rendering | "good" | "good" | "good enough" | "good" | "Good enough" adds interpretive nuance not in the Greek |
